subject: Physics
q:
A transmitting antenna is kept on the surface of the earth. The minimum height of receiving antenna required to receive the signal in line of sight at 4 km distance from it is x×10-2 m. The value of x is _____.
(Let, radius of earth R=6400 km)
question:
A transmitting antenna is kept on the surface of the earth. The minimum height of receiving antenna required to receive the signal in line of sight at 4 km distance from it is x×10-2 m. The value of x is _____.
(Let, radius of earth R=6400 km)
solution:
The formula to calculate the range of the antenna is given by

γ=2Rh   ...1

Substitute the values of the known parameters into equation (1) and solve to obtain the required height of the antenna.

4×103=2×6400000×h⇒16×106=2×64×105×h⇒h=1602×64m=108m=10008×10-2 m=125×10-2 m

question:
Match List-I with List-II

 

List-I

(Layer of atmosphere)

  

List-II

(Approximate height over earth's surface)
(A) F1-Layer (I) 10 km
B D-Layer II 170-190 km
C Troposphere III 100 km
D E-Layer V 65-75 km

Choose the correct answer from the options given below.
solution:
The F1 layer is above the E layer and the ionization is less compared to E layer. It ranges to 170-190 km.

The D layer is located above the stratosphere. It ranges from 60-90 km above the earth's surface.

The troposphere starts from the ground and ranges to 100 km.

The E layer is located above the D layer. The intensity of ionization is less than the D layer. It ranges from 100-125 km above the earth's surface.

subject: Physics
q: The modulation index for an A.M. wave having maximum and minimum peak to peak voltages of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>14</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mV</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mV</mi></math> respectively is :
question: The modulation index for an A.M. wave having maximum and minimum peak to peak voltages of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>14</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mV</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mV</mi></math> respectively is :
solution:
As we know, modulation index is given by,

μ=Amax-AminAmax+Amin

=14-614+6=0.4

subject: Physics
q: A radar has a power of $1 \mathrm{~Kw}$ and is operating at a frequency of $10 \mathrm{~GHz}$. It is located on a mountain top of height $500 \mathrm{~m}$. The maximum distance upto which it can detect object located on the surface of the earth (Radius of earth $=6.4 \times 10^6 \mathrm{~m}$ ) is
question: A radar has a power of $1 \mathrm{~Kw}$ and is operating at a frequency of $10 \mathrm{~GHz}$. It is located on a mountain top of height $500 \mathrm{~m}$. The maximum distance upto which it can detect object located on the surface of the earth (Radius of earth $=6.4 \times 10^6 \mathrm{~m}$ ) is
solution: Maximum distance on earth where object can be detected is $\mathrm{d}$, then $\begin{array}{ll} & (h+R)^2=d^2+R^2 \\ \Rightarrow & d^2=h^2+2 R h \\ \text { since } & h < < R, \quad \Rightarrow d^2=2 h R \\ \Rightarrow & d=\sqrt{2(500)\left(6.4 \times 10^6\right)}=80 \mathrm{~km} \end{array}$

subject: Physics
q: A signal of 5 kHz frequency is amplitude modulated on a carrier wave of frequency 2 MHz. The frequencies of the resultant signal is/are:
question: A signal of 5 kHz frequency is amplitude modulated on a carrier wave of frequency 2 MHz. The frequencies of the resultant signal is/are:
solution:
Frequency of signal wave =5 kHz=f. Carrier wave frequency =2MHz

=2000kHz=fc. The resultant signal maximum frequency=f+fc

= 5 + 2 0 0 0 kHz

= 2 0 0 5 kHz

Resultant signal minimum frequency

=fc-f

= 2 0 0 0 - 5 kHz

= 1995 kHz

q: A signal of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mi>THz</mi></math> frequency can be transmitted with maximum efficiency by
question: A signal of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mi>THz</mi></math> frequency can be transmitted with maximum efficiency by
solution: Fiber optics, or optical fiber, refers to the technology that transmits information as light pulses along a glass or plastic fiber. Fiber optics have high efficiency of data transmission.

subject: Physics
q: Which of the following frequencies does not belong to FM broadcast.
question: Which of the following frequencies does not belong to FM broadcast.
solution:
Sound waves of different frequencies can be used in different purposes. FM broadcasting frequently makes use of frequency modulation. Additionally, it is utilised in telemetry, radar, seismic prospecting, EEG-based seizure monitoring of neonates, two-way radio systems, sound synthesis, magnetic tape recording, and some video transmission systems.

The range for FM broadcasting lies between 88 MHz to 108 MHz.

Hence, 64 MHz does not belong to the FM broadcast.

subject: Physics
q: To radiate EM signal of wavelength <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>λ</mi></math> with high efficiency, the antennas should have a minimum size equal to:
question: To radiate EM signal of wavelength <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>λ</mi></math> with high efficiency, the antennas should have a minimum size equal to:
solution: For efficient radiation and reception, the height of transmitting and receiving antennas should be comparable to a quarter of wavelength of the frequency used. So for efficient transmission of signals of wavelengths λ the minimum length of antenna should be λ4.

subject: Physics
q: What should be the height of transmitting antenna and the population covered if the television telecast is to cover a radius of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>150</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi><mo>?</mo></math>  The average population density around the tower is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2000</mn><mo> </mo><msup><mi>km</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> and the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>R</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>6</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo>.</mo></math>
question: What should be the height of transmitting antenna and the population covered if the television telecast is to cover a radius of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>150</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi><mo>?</mo></math>  The average population density around the tower is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2000</mn><mo> </mo><msup><mi>km</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> and the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>R</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>6</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo>.</mo></math>
solution:
Radius covered, r=2RHT
150 km=2×6.5×106 mHT
150 km×1032=2×6.5×106HT
HT=1731 m

Population covered =πr22000 km-2
=3.14×(150)2×2000=1413×105

subject: Physics
q: A radio can tune to any station in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mi>MHz</mi></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mi>MHz</mi></math> band. The value of corresponding wavelength bandwidth will be
question: A radio can tune to any station in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mi>MHz</mi></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mi>MHz</mi></math> band. The value of corresponding wavelength bandwidth will be
solution:
Given: Frequency f1=6MHz

Frequency f2=10MHz

Wavelength can be calculated using λ=cf.

⇒λ1=cf1=3×1086×106=50 m

⇒λ2=cf2=3×10810×106=30 m

Wavelength bandwidth is the difference of upper lower wavelengths =λ1-λ2=20 m

subject: Physics
q: A transmitting antenna has a height of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>320</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> and that of receiving antenna is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2000</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The maximum distance between them for satisfactory communication in line of sight mode is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>d</mi></math>. The value of  <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>d</mi></math> is ________ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>km</mi><mo>.</mo></math>
question: A transmitting antenna has a height of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>320</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> and that of receiving antenna is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2000</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The maximum distance between them for satisfactory communication in line of sight mode is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>d</mi></math>. The value of  <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>d</mi></math> is ________ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>km</mi><mo>.</mo></math>
solution:
Recall the formula of maximum distance between transmitting antenna and receiving antenna for satisfactory communication in terms of the radius of the earth and height of antennas, 

d=2Rh1+2Rh2⇒d=2×6400×0.32+2×6400×2≈224 km

subject: Physics
q:
The power radiated from a linear antenna of length l is proportional to
(Given, λ= Wavelength of wave):
question:
The power radiated from a linear antenna of length l is proportional to
(Given, λ= Wavelength of wave):
solution:
Linear antennas are considered to be those antennas that imply the use of electrically thin conductors having diameter much less than the wavelength of the electromagnetic wave passing through them.

The formula to calculate the effective power radiated by a linear antenna is given by

Peff=kl2λ2

where, k is a constant.

subject: Physics
q: A $10 \mathrm{~kW}$ transmitter emits radio waves of wavelength $500 \mathrm{~m}$. The number of photons emitted per second by the transmitter is of the order of
question: A $10 \mathrm{~kW}$ transmitter emits radio waves of wavelength $500 \mathrm{~m}$. The number of photons emitted per second by the transmitter is of the order of
solution:
Power $=\frac{n h c}{\lambda}$
(where, $n=$ no. of photons per second)
$$
\Rightarrow \quad n=\frac{10 \times 10^3 \times 500}{6.6 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^8} \simeq 10^{31}
$$

question: In a communication system operating at wavelength <math><mn>800</mn><mi> </mi><mi>n</mi><mi>m</mi><mo>,</mo></math> only one percent of source frequency is available as signal bandwidth. The number of channels accommodated for transmitting <math><mi>T</mi><mi>V</mi></math> signals of band width <math><mn>6</mn><mi> </mi><mi>M</mi><mi>H</mi><mi>z</mi></math> are (Take velocity of light <math><mi>c</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>8</mn></mrow></msup><mi> </mi><mrow><mrow><mi>m</mi></mrow><mo>/</mo><mrow><mi>s</mi></mrow></mrow><mo>,</mo><mi> </mi><mi>h</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>6.6</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>34</mn></mrow></msup><mi> </mi><mi>J</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">-</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"><mi>s</mi></mi></math> )
solution:
Available signal width =1100×cλ

=1100×3×108800×10-9 Hz=3.75×1012 Hz

Number of available channels =3.75×10126×106

=6.25×105

subject: Physics
q: A baseband signal of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mi>MHz</mi></math> frequency is modulated with a carrier signal of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mi>GHz</mi></math> frequency using amplitude modulation method. What should be the minimum size of antenna required to transmit the modulated signal ?
question: A baseband signal of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mi>MHz</mi></math> frequency is modulated with a carrier signal of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mi>GHz</mi></math> frequency using amplitude modulation method. What should be the minimum size of antenna required to transmit the modulated signal ?
solution:
The wavelength of the carrier wave is,

λc=cνc=3×1083.5×109=335 m.

The minimum size of the antenna required is 14th of the wavelength of the carrier. Therefore,

l=14×335=21.4 mm

subject: Physics
q: A transmitting antenna at the top of a tower has a height 32 m and the height of the receiving antenna is 50 m. What is the maximum distance between them for satisfactory communication in line of sight (LOS) mode?
question: A transmitting antenna at the top of a tower has a height 32 m and the height of the receiving antenna is 50 m. What is the maximum distance between them for satisfactory communication in line of sight (LOS) mode?
solution:
Given,

Height of transmitting antenna is, hT=50 m

Height of receiving antenna is, hR=32 m

Thus, maximum distance for satisfactory communication will be,

dM=2RhT+2RhR where R is radius of the Earth,

dM=2RhT+2RhRdM=2×64×105×50+2×64×105×32dM=800010+640010dM=1440010 mdM=45536.7 mdM=45.5 km

subject: Physics

[use radius of the earth=6400 km]
question:

[use radius of the earth=6400 km]
solution: The formula of range of the antenna is given by the formula,=2Rh1+2Rh2., here R is the radius of the earth then, putting the values, the range =57.28 km.

subject: Physics
q: The maximum and minimum voltage of an amplitude modulated signal are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> respectively. The percentage modulation index will be
question: The maximum and minimum voltage of an amplitude modulated signal are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> respectively. The percentage modulation index will be
solution:
If amplitude of carrier wave is Ac and amplitude of message wave is Am,

Amax=Ac+Am and Amin=Ac-Am

Modulation index,

Mi=AmAc=Amax-Amin2Amax+Amin2=60-2060+20=12=50%

subject: Physics
q: To double the covering range of a TV transmitting tower, its height should be multiplied by:
question: To double the covering range of a TV transmitting tower, its height should be multiplied by:
solution:
The range of a tower in the communication system is given by

Range=2hR

Here, h= Height of the tower,

R= radius of Earth

Given, The range of tower is to be doubled.

As, Range ∝h, to make the range double, the height h have to be made 4 times.

subject: Physics
q: If a carrier wave $\mathrm{c}(\mathrm{t})=\mathrm{A} \sin \omega_{\mathrm{c}} \mathrm{t}$ is amplitude modulated by a modulator signal $\mathrm{m}(\mathrm{t})=\mathrm{A}$ sin $\omega_{\mathrm{m}} \mathrm{t}$ then the equation of modulated signal $\left[\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{m}}(\mathrm{t})\right]$ and its modulation index are respectively
question: If a carrier wave $\mathrm{c}(\mathrm{t})=\mathrm{A} \sin \omega_{\mathrm{c}} \mathrm{t}$ is amplitude modulated by a modulator signal $\mathrm{m}(\mathrm{t})=\mathrm{A}$ sin $\omega_{\mathrm{m}} \mathrm{t}$ then the equation of modulated signal $\left[\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{m}}(\mathrm{t})\right]$ and its modulation index are respectively
solution:
Modulation index
$$
\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{a}}=\frac{\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{m}}}{\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{c}}}=\frac{\mathrm{A}}{\mathrm{A}}=1
$$
Equation of modulated signal $\left[\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{m}}(\mathrm{t})\right]$
$$
=\mathrm{E}_{(\mathrm{C})}+\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{a}} \mathrm{E}_{(\mathrm{C})} \sin \omega_{\mathrm{m}}^{\mathrm{t}}
$$
$=\mathrm{A}\left(1+\sin \mathrm{w}_{\mathrm{C}} \mathrm{t}\right) \sin \omega_{\mathrm{m}} \mathrm{t}$
$\left(\right.$ As $\left.\mathrm{E}_{(\mathrm{C})}=\mathrm{A} \sin \omega_{\mathrm{c}} \mathrm{t}\right)$

(i) ground level
(ii) a height of 5 m.
The increase in antenna range in case (ii) relative to case (i) is n%. The value of n, to the nearest integer, is.
question:
A TV transmission tower antenna is at a height of 20 m. Suppose that the receiving antenna is at.
(i) ground level
(ii) a height of 5 m.
The increase in antenna range in case (ii) relative to case (i) is n%. The value of n, to the nearest integer, is.
solution:
Range =2Rh

Range (i)=2Rh

Range (ii) =2Rh+2Rh'

where h=20 m & h'=5 m

Ans =2Rh'2Rh×100%=520×100%=50%

subject: Physics
q: In satellite communication, the uplink frequency band used is:
question: In satellite communication, the uplink frequency band used is:
solution: In satellite telecommunication, a downlink is the link from a satellite down to one or more ground stations or receivers, and an uplink is the link from a ground station up to a satellite. Uplink frequency band for satellite communication is 5.925-6.425 GHz.

subject: Physics
q: The required height of a TV tower which can cover the population of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>03</mn></math> lakh is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>h</mi></math>. If the average population density is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo></math>per square <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>km</mi></math> and the radius of earth is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6400</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi></math>, then the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>h</mi></math> will be _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>.
question: The required height of a TV tower which can cover the population of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>03</mn></math> lakh is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>h</mi></math>. If the average population density is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo></math>per square <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>km</mi></math> and the radius of earth is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6400</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi></math>, then the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>h</mi></math> will be _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>.
solution:
Maximum range of TV transmission d=2Rh

d=2×6400×h×10-3 (here, h in m)

Maximum area of TV transmission

Area =πd2

=π×2×6400×h×10-3 km2

Now, 6.03×100000=100×π×2×6400×10-3 h

h=6.03×10510×π×128

h=150 m

subject: Physics
q: A signal of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kW</mi></math> is transmitted in a cable. The attenuation of cable is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>dB</mi></math> per <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>km</mi></math> and cable length is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The power received at the receiver is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mi>x</mi></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">W</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is _________. [Gain in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>dB</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><msub><mi>log</mi><mn>10</mn></msub><mfenced><mfrac><msub><mi>P</mi><mi>O</mi></msub><msub><mi>P</mi><mi>i</mi></msub></mfrac></mfenced></math>]
question: A signal of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kW</mi></math> is transmitted in a cable. The attenuation of cable is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>dB</mi></math> per <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>km</mi></math> and cable length is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The power received at the receiver is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mi>x</mi></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">W</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is _________. [Gain in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>dB</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><msub><mi>log</mi><mn>10</mn></msub><mfenced><mfrac><msub><mi>P</mi><mi>O</mi></msub><msub><mi>P</mi><mi>i</mi></msub></mfrac></mfenced></math>]
solution:
Sound level decreases by 5 dB every km so, sound level decreased in 20 km=100 dB

β2-β1=10 log10I2I1

-100=10 log10I2I1⇒I1I2=1010

I2=10-10I1⇒P2=10-10P1=10-8 W

x=8

subject: Physics
q: The maximum and minimum amplitude of an amplitude modulated wave is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>16</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> respectively. The modulation index for this amplitude modulated wave is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is _______. (Round off your answer to the nearest integer)
question: The maximum and minimum amplitude of an amplitude modulated wave is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>16</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> respectively. The modulation index for this amplitude modulated wave is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is _______. (Round off your answer to the nearest integer)
solution:
Modulation index =Amax-AminAmax+Amin

=16-816+8=824=13=0.33

x×10-2=0.33

So, $x=33$
